comment_text.iti83:2.2: Kāye dubbaṇṇiyaṁ okkamati doesn’t refer to the dimming of a divine radiance. It describes a declining physical appearance (AN 5.50:9.4).

root_text.iti83:2.1: “Yadā, bhikkhave, devo devakāyā cavanadhammo hoti, pañcassa pubbanimittāni pātubhavanti—
root_text.iti83:2.2: mālā milāyanti, vatthāni kilissanti, kacchehi sedā muccanti, kāye dubbaṇṇiyaṁ okkamati, sake devo devāsane nābhiramatīti.
root_text.iti83:2.3: Tamenaṁ, bhikkhave, devā ‘cavanadhammo ayaṁ devaputto’ti iti viditvā tīhi vācāhi anumodenti:
root_text.iti83:5.1: Evaṁ vutte, aññataro bhikkhu bhagavantaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.iti83:9.1: Yaṁ manussabhūto samāno tathāgatappavedite dhammavinaye saddhaṁ paṭilabhati;
root_text.iti83:10.1: Sā kho panassa saddhā niviṭṭhā hoti mūlajātā patiṭṭhitā daḷhā asaṁhāriyā samaṇena vā brāhmaṇena vā devena vā mārena vā brahmunā vā kenaci vā lokasmiṁ;
root_text.iti83:19.1: Ayampi attho vutto bhagavatā, iti me sutanti.

translation_text.iti83:1.1: This was said by the Buddha, the Perfected One: that is what I heard.
translation_text.iti83:2.1: “Mendicants, when a god is due to pass away from the realm of the gods, five harbingers appear.
translation_text.iti83:2.2: Their flower-garlands wither; their clothes become soiled; they sweat from the armpits; their physical appearance deteriorates; and they no longer delight in their heavenly throne.
translation_text.iti83:2.3: When the other gods know that that godling is due to pass away, they wish them well in three ways:
translation_text.iti83:2.4: ‘Sir, may you go from here to a good place!
translation_text.iti83:3.1: When you have gone to a good place, may you be blessed with good fortune!
translation_text.iti83:4.1: When you have been blessed with good fortune, may you become well grounded!’”
translation_text.iti83:5.1: When he said this, one of the mendicants said to the Buddha,
translation_text.iti83:5.2: “Sir, what do the gods reckon to be going to a good place?
translation_text.iti83:6.1: What do they reckon to be blessed with good fortune?
translation_text.iti83:7.1: What do they reckon to become well grounded?”
translation_text.iti83:8.1: “It is human existence, mendicant, that the gods reckon to be going to a good place.
translation_text.iti83:9.1: When a human being gains faith in the teaching and training proclaimed by the Realized One,
translation_text.iti83:9.2: that is what the gods reckon to be blessed with good fortune.
translation_text.iti83:10.1: When that faith in the Realized One is settled, rooted, and planted deep; when it’s strong and can’t be shifted by any ascetic or brahmin or god or Māra or divinity or by anyone in the world,
translation_text.iti83:10.2: that is what the gods reckon to become well grounded.”
translation_text.iti83:12.2: a god passes from the realm of the gods,
translation_text.iti83:13.1: ‘Go, worthy sir, from here to a good place,
translation_text.iti83:13.3: As a human being, gain supreme faith
translation_text.iti83:14.1: May that faith of yours be settled,
translation_text.iti83:14.4: in the true teaching so well proclaimed.
translation_text.iti83:17.1: then, having made much worldly merit
translation_text.iti83:17.4: in the true teaching, the spiritual life.’
translation_text.iti83:18.1: It is due to such sympathy
translation_text.iti83:18.2: that when the gods know a god
translation_text.iti83:18.3: is due to pass away, they wish them well:
translation_text.iti83:19.1: This too is a matter that was spoken by the Blessed One: that is what I heard.

variant_text.iti83:9.2: idaṁ kho → idaṁ kho pana (bj) | bhikkhu → bhikkhave (bj, sya-all, pts-vp-pli1)
